name: REPLACE: Descriptive name for this financial stress test (e.g., 'Emerging Market FX Crisis Stress Test')
description: REPLACE: 2-3 sentence description of the economy, its financial system, and the shock scenario being tested.

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the central bank (e.g., 'Central Bank of XYZ')
actors.description: REPLACE: The monetary authority — its mandate (inflation targeting, exchange rate management, financial stability), key policy instruments, and current policy stance.
objectives: Maintain price stability within target band
Ensure financial system stability and prevent systemic failures
REPLACE: Exchange rate objective (e.g., 'Defend managed float' or 'Allow orderly adjustment')
Preserve adequate foreign reserve coverage
constraints: REPLACE: Key policy constraint (e.g., 'Inflation already above target limits rate cut room')
REPLACE: Reserve constraint (e.g., 'Net reserves provide X months of import cover')
Political pressure may conflict with optimal monetary policy
Transmission mechanism weakened by dollarisation or informal economy
available_actions: Adjust policy interest rate (raise, hold, or cut)
Intervene in foreign exchange market (sell or buy reserves)
Adjust reserve requirements for commercial banks
Activate emergency liquidity assistance facility
Tighten or loosen macro-prudential regulations
Issue forward guidance to manage market expectations
Coordinate fiscal-monetary policy with government
Engage international institutions for technical or financial support
Impose or relax capital flow management measures
Conduct stress tests and publish results

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the largest commercial bank
actors.description: REPLACE: Profile of the dominant bank — asset size, market share, capital adequacy, asset quality, and key exposures (sector, FX, sovereign).
objectives: Maintain profitability and shareholder returns
Preserve capital ratios above regulatory minimums
REPLACE: Bank-specific strategic objective
Retain depositor and creditor confidence
constraints: REPLACE: Asset quality concern (e.g., 'NPL ratio rising in sector X exposure')
REPLACE: Liquidity constraint (e.g., 'FX liquidity dependent on central bank allocation')
Interbank exposures create contagion risk from peer failures
Regulatory requirements limit balance sheet flexibility
available_actions: Tighten credit standards and reduce new lending
Increase deposit rates to attract funding
Sell liquid assets to raise cash buffers
Request central bank liquidity support
Accelerate provisioning on deteriorating loan book
Reduce foreign currency lending exposure
Issue public confidence statement to depositors
Negotiate with large depositors for term extension
Reduce dividend to preserve capital
Seek strategic investor or capital injection

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the second-largest bank or a bank with distinct risk profile
actors.description: REPLACE: Profile of a second major bank — how it differs from bank A in terms of ownership (state-owned? foreign-owned?), risk appetite, and exposures.
objectives: REPLACE: Bank B's primary objective (may differ due to ownership structure)
Maintain competitive position relative to bank A
Preserve capital and liquidity under stress
REPLACE: Bank-specific growth or stability objective
constraints: REPLACE: Ownership-related constraint (e.g., 'Government ownership creates directed lending pressure')
REPLACE: Specific vulnerability (e.g., 'Higher concentration in real estate lending')
Thinner capital buffers than bank A
Less diversified funding base
available_actions: Request government capital injection (if state-owned)
Tighten credit underwriting standards
Prioritise FX allocation to strategic sectors
Coordinate with bank A on interbank market stability
Accelerate digital cost reduction programme
Seek parent company support (if foreign-owned)
Issue subordinated debt to shore up capital
Merge or acquire weaker peer for scale
Reduce branch network to cut operating costs
Negotiate debt restructuring with problem borrowers

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the fiscal authority (e.g., 'Ministry of Finance and Planning')
actors.description: REPLACE: The government's fiscal authority — budget position, debt levels, revenue dependence on any single source, and political constraints.
objectives: Maintain fiscal sustainability and debt serviceability
REPLACE: Revenue objective (e.g., 'Close fiscal gap from commodity revenue decline')
Preserve sovereign credit rating
Maintain social stability through continued public spending
constraints: REPLACE: Revenue constraint (e.g., 'Commodity revenues declining X%')
REPLACE: Debt constraint (e.g., 'Debt-to-GDP at X%, approaching sustainability threshold')
Election cycle limits politically difficult measures
Public sector wage bill is rigid and large
available_actions: Draw on sovereign wealth or stabilisation fund
Issue domestic government bonds to fund deficit
Issue international sovereign bonds (if market access exists)
Implement revenue measures (tax increases, base broadening)
Cut capital expenditure while protecting social transfers
Negotiate with IMF for programme or precautionary facility
Announce economic reform programme to signal commitment
Provide fiscal backstop to banking system if needed
Restructure or extend public debt maturities
Coordinate fiscal response with central bank monetary policy

actors.name: Retail Depositor Segment
actors.description: REPLACE: Profile of retail depositors — aggregate deposit size, financial literacy, historical experience with banking crises, and information sources.
objectives: Preserve real value of savings
Maintain access to banking services and liquidity
Protect against currency depreciation
Avoid being caught in a bank failure
constraints: Limited financial sophistication — rely on media signals and herd behaviour
FX purchase limits restrict ability to hedge currency risk
Deposit insurance may be absent, limited, or not trusted
Concentrated savings in domestic currency with no natural hedge
available_actions: Maintain deposits at current bank (status quo)
Move deposits to perceived safer bank
Convert savings to foreign currency at official rate (if available)
Convert savings via parallel market or informal channels
Withdraw cash and hold outside banking system
Purchase durable goods or real estate as inflation hedge
Reduce discretionary spending in anticipation of crisis
Queue at banks for FX allocation
Spread information and rumours through social networks

actors.name: REPLACE: Name for institutional depositor segment (e.g., 'Corporate Treasurers & Pension Funds')
actors.description: REPLACE: Profile of large institutional depositors — pension funds, insurers, corporate treasuries. Their FX needs, hedging sophistication, and panic threshold.
objectives: Protect portfolio value and meet fiduciary obligations
Secure FX for trade finance and operational needs
Hedge exposure to currency and interest rate movements
Maintain business continuity despite financial market stress
constraints: Investment mandates and regulations limit portfolio flexibility
FX hedging instruments may be thin or unavailable in local market
Large transactions attract regulatory attention and allocation queues
Counterparty credit concerns limit interbank and repo activity
available_actions: Negotiate priority FX allocation with relationship banks
Reduce import volumes or defer discretionary FX payments
Shift portfolio allocation toward foreign-currency-earning assets
Lobby central bank and government through industry associations
Hoard FX when available (precautionary behaviour)
Seek offshore hedging through NDF market
Delay dividend repatriation to reduce FX demand
Issue public statements calling for policy clarity

actors.name: Foreign Portfolio & Direct Investors
actors.description: REPLACE: Profile of foreign investors — estimated holdings (government bonds, equities, FDI), sensitivity to credit rating changes, and flight triggers.
objectives: Protect USD-denominated returns on country portfolio
Maintain ability to repatriate capital without restrictions
Avoid being trapped by capital controls or FX rationing
Reassess country allocation relative to peer alternatives
constraints: Exit requires central bank FX liquidity that may not be available at scale
Fire-sale of domestic bonds depresses prices and crystalises losses
Investment mandate may restrict rapid reallocation
Reputational risk of being seen to trigger or worsen a crisis
available_actions: Maintain current positions and monitor developments
Gradually reduce exposure through secondary market sales
Request FX allocation for bond maturity repatriation
Engage credit rating agencies to flag sovereign concerns
Shift new allocations to alternative emerging markets
Hedge country exposure through offshore instruments
Lobby central bank governor for repatriation assurances
Publish research notes that influence international sentiment

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of relevant multilateral (e.g., 'International Monetary Fund')
actors.description: REPLACE: The relevant international financial institution — its relationship with the country, prior programme history, and likely conditionality framework.
objectives: Promote macroeconomic stability and sustainable growth
Advocate for policy reforms that address structural vulnerabilities
Prevent contagion to regional neighbours
Maintain institutional credibility through consistent conditionality
constraints: Cannot intervene without formal government request
Programme conditionality must follow established frameworks
Political sensitivity in borrowing country — IMF stigma
Must coordinate with other multilaterals and bilateral creditors
available_actions: Publish Article IV staff report with policy recommendations
Offer precautionary credit line or stand-by arrangement
Provide technical assistance on FX regime, fiscal framework, or bank supervision
Issue public or private statements on country outlook
Coordinate with regional development banks on complementary support
Recommend structural benchmarks as programme conditionality
Facilitate debt sustainability analysis
Engage in confidential policy dialogue with authorities

actors.name: International Credit Rating Agencies
actors.description: The major credit rating agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch) that assess the sovereign and bank credit quality. Their actions directly affect foreign investor behaviour, sovereign borrowing costs, and public confidence. Tend to be lagging indicators but their announcements create step-change market reactions.
objectives: Maintain analytical credibility through accurate and timely assessments
Provide early warning of deteriorating creditworthiness
Balance responsiveness with stability (avoid pro-cyclical rating volatility)
Comply with regulatory expectations for rating methodology
constraints: Rating methodology creates inertia — changes require formal committee process
Criticism for being either too slow (late to downgrade) or too fast (triggering crises)
Access to confidential data depends on cooperation of rated entities
Must maintain independence from rated sovereign and bank interests
available_actions: Affirm current rating with stable outlook
Place rating on negative watch (review for downgrade)
Downgrade sovereign or bank rating by one or more notches
Change outlook from stable to negative without rating change
Publish special commentary on emerging risks
Upgrade outlook if policy response is credible
Initiate unsolicited review of bank ratings
Engage in dialogue with authorities to assess policy credibility

relationships.description: Central bank is the prudential regulator and lender of last resort
Regulatory oversight; ownership structure may add political dimension
Fiscal-monetary policy coordination; operational independence varies by jurisdiction
Interbank lending, payment system linkages, and competitive dynamics
Primary banking relationship for large share of retail depositors
Corporate banking and custodian services for institutional clients
Secondary banking franchise; perceived government backing may provide confidence
Government appoints central bank leadership; fiscal needs create implicit pressure
Foreign investor repatriation demands create direct FX reserve pressure
Rating actions directly influence foreign investor allocation decisions
Rating agency evaluates central bank policy credibility
Sovereign rating directly reflects fiscal sustainability assessment
Policy surveillance, technical assistance, and potential programme engagement
Fiscal surveillance and debt sustainability monitoring
Retail panic can be triggered by institutional withdrawal signals, and vice versa
Corporate sector lobbies central bank for improved FX allocation

shock_scenario.trigger: REPLACE: Description of the triggering event (e.g., 'Commodity price collapse reduces export revenues by 40%', 'Major trading partner imposes sanctions', 'Largest bank reveals hidden NPL exposure')

timeline.description: REPLACE: Initial shock manifestation — what becomes visible first and to whom
REPLACE: How stress propagates — contagion channels, confidence erosion, policy response lag
REPLACE: Maximum stress scenario — what metrics hit critical thresholds
REPLACE: How the crisis resolves — policy response crystallises, system stabilises or deepens

prediction_questions: Will the central bank raise or cut interest rates in response to the shock?
Will any bank require emergency liquidity assistance?
Will foreign investor capital outflows exceed available reserves?
Will the government need to seek IMF assistance?
Will the sovereign credit rating be downgraded?
Will the currency depreciate by more than 20% from pre-shock levels?
Will retail depositor behaviour trigger a bank run?
Will the banking system's aggregate NPL ratio exceed the stress threshold?
